Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Kazimierz Twardowski

1866 – 1938

Content and object in presentations: the Polish school’s founding contribution.

Kazimierz Twardowski on mind, psychology, and the soul: an in-depth reconstruction

Twardowski did not leave a single, unified “philosophy of mind” treatise. The view has to be reconstructed from several clusters of writings: above all On the Content and Object of Presentations (1894), Psychology vs. Physiology and Philosophy (1897), On the Classification of Mental Phenomena (1898), On the So-Called Relative Truths (1900), Actions and Products (1912), his work on concepts and judgments, and the early metaphysical pieces on the soul and immortality. That reconstruction must also remain somewhat provisional, because an important part of Twardowski’s corpus consists of lecture manuscripts, and the Stanford Encyclopedia notes that assessments based only on his published works remain incomplete in light of those manuscripts. Still, the main architecture is clear: Twardowski offers a realist, intentionalist, descriptive-psychological theory of mind, later combined with an anti-psychologistic theory of logic and meaning, and accompanied by an early metaphysics of the soul as the non-corporeal subject of mental phenomena.

The central thesis of this report is this: Twardowski’s mind is not primarily a Cartesian inner theatre and not a physical mechanism; it is a structured domain of psychic acts, contents, objects, products, and signs. Mental life is intentional: acts are directed beyond themselves toward objects through contents. Psychology studies these acts and their products by descriptive analysis grounded in inner experience. Logic and semantics, however, cannot simply be reduced to psychology, because judgments, meanings, and truths must be treated at the level of products and sign-expressions. Finally, in his soul writings, Twardowski adds a metaphysical claim: mental phenomena require a single subject or self, understood as a non-material, non-corporeal substance, and he argues against both materialist and bundle-theoretic accounts of the self.

1. Historical location: Brentano’s student, but not merely a Brentanian

Twardowski stands at a crucial turning point in the history of philosophy of mind. He was a student of Franz Brentano and the founder of the Lvov-Warsaw School. His most influential work, On the Content and Object of Presentations, clarified the distinction between the content and the object of a mental act inside Brentanian intentionality theory. The distinction became important not only for Brentanians, but also for Husserl, Meinong, and, indirectly through G. F. Stout, for the transition of Moore and Russell away from idealism toward analytic philosophy.

The intellectual background is late nineteenth-century psychology, where two broad currents were in tension: Wundtian experimental psychology and Brentanian descriptive psychology. Twardowski sided chiefly with Brentano’s descriptive project, but he did not reject empirical work: he lectured on experimental topics such as optical illusion and established the first empirical psychology laboratory in Poland. This is important because Twardowski is not a speculative metaphysician who ignores empirical psychology; nor is he a reductionist who thinks psychology collapses into physiology. He treats psychology as an empirical and descriptive discipline, grounded in experience, especially inner experience, but methodologically connected with analysis, logic, and philosophy.

His relation to Brentano is one of inheritance and correction. From Brentano he accepts the primacy of intentionality, the importance of presentations, the sui generis character of judgment, and the use of descriptive analysis. But he modifies Brentano in three decisive ways: first, by separating content from object; second, by later splitting Brentano’s single class of “love and hate” into feelings and will; third, by eventually rejecting psychologism in logic while preserving psychology as a crucial philosophical propaedeutic.

2. What psychology studies: psychic life, psychic facts, and descriptive analysis

Twardowski’s psychology studies psychic life. In his earlier Brentanian language this means mental phenomena; later he prefers terms such as psychic facts, psychic functions, psychic products, and psychic dispositions. Psychic facts are non-spatial and are directly given in inner experience, whereas natural objects are spatial and given through outer sense. Psychology is therefore not a branch of physiology, even if physiological knowledge may be relevant; its object is not the brain as a physical object, but the internally accessible domain of mental facts, functions, and products.

The method is descriptive-analytic. Twardowski treats psychic life as a concrete whole whose parts and elements can be distinguished through abstraction. This is one of the deepest continuities between his psychology and his ontology: the mind is not a heap of unrelated episodes but a structured whole, and psychological analysis proceeds by decomposing this whole into acts, contents, modes, objects, and products. Płotka’s reconstruction of the Twardowskian school emphasizes that this descriptive attitude and mereological background remained common threads connecting Brentano, Twardowski, and many of Twardowski’s students.

Introspection has high epistemic value for Twardowski. Inner experience is non-sensual and self-evident; it consists in perceiving one’s own mental facts. Rechlewicz summarizes Twardowski’s position as the view that introspection plays a fundamental role in psychology, which Twardowski treated as a primary philosophical discipline, and that grounding philosophy in inner experience promised universal and justified results. But Twardowski’s position is not naïve introspectionism. He distinguishes inner perception from inner observation: inner perception is direct and self-evident, whereas inner observation is more complex, attention-laden, and fallible. Since mental facts pass quickly and are altered by attention, memory and retrospective reconstruction become methodologically important.

This produces a distinctive epistemology of mind. First-person access is privileged, but psychology cannot be only a diary of private experience. To become a science, it must classify psychic facts, compare them, formulate generalizations, and use signs, reports, memory, and experimental procedures where appropriate. Twardowski’s psychology is therefore empirical, introspective, classificatory, and analytic at once.

3. Intentionality: the act-content-object triad

The core of Twardowski’s philosophy of mind is his correction of Brentano’s intentionality thesis. Brentano’s thesis, in simplified form, is that every mental phenomenon is directed toward an object. Twardowski accepts this, but argues that Brentano’s terminology confused two things: the content through which an object is presented and the object that is presented. Twardowski’s own thesis is that every mental phenomenon has both content and object, and that the act is directed toward the object, not toward the content.

The three terms have sharply different roles. The act is the mental event itself: the presenting, judging, feeling, or willing that occurs at a time. The content is internal to the mind and depends on the act; it is that through which the object is presented. The object is what the act is about or directed toward; normally it is not inside the mind and is independent of the act, though a content can itself become an object in a second-order act, as when we reflect on the content of a presentation.

This is not a minor terminological point. It is Twardowski’s answer to a fundamental problem of intentionality: when I think about something absent, fictional, impossible, or nonexistent, what exactly am I thinking about? If the object of thought is an inner mental item, then negative existential judgment becomes absurd: to judge that the aether does not exist would be to reject something that exists in my mind. Twardowski avoids this by saying: the mental content exists in me, but the object intended through it need not exist. The act is psychologically real; the content is mentally real; the object may or may not exist.

This makes Twardowski a realist about intentional objects in a technical sense, but not a naïve realist. The object is not necessarily an existing thing; it is whatever can be presented or named. Mental directedness is therefore broader than successful reference to an existing entity. This is one reason his theory became so important for Meinong’s object theory and Husserl’s phenomenology.

4. Presentations and nonexistent objects

Presentations are foundational for Twardowski. A judgment, feeling, or volition normally presupposes that something is presented. This follows Brentano’s view that mental phenomena are either presentations or founded on presentations. But Twardowski radicalizes the idea: every presentation has an object, even when that object does not exist. There are no objectless presentations; there are only presentations whose objects fail to exist.

The standard examples are impossible or contradictory objects, such as a round square, and merely nonexistent objects, such as the aether or Pegasus. In such cases the act and content exist, but the object does not. The object, not the content, is what is denied in a negative existential judgment. This permits Twardowski to say that the round square is what is presented and what is denied to exist, while also insisting that the content through which it is presented exists as a mental component of the act.

This doctrine has two important consequences. First, it gives a powerful account of thought about fiction, error, impossibility, and empty names. Second, it generates a metaphysics of objects broader than a metaphysics of existents. To be an object is to be capable of presentation or naming; existence is a further status that some objects have and others lack. The cost is that the theory is vulnerable to the Russellian objection that it overpopulates ontology with strange objects; the SEP notes that, in Meinong’s later version, this kind of theory became a target of Russell’s “On Denoting.”

5. The structure of content: elements, marks, and the impossibility of exhaustive presentation

Twardowski’s content-object distinction is embedded in a broader mereology. The content of a presentation has elements; the object has characteristic marks or parts. The elements of the content present the characteristic marks of the object. Thus, when I present a table, some features of the table are represented in my content, but not all. The table has indefinitely many aspects, relations, and parts; my presentation contains only a finite selection.

This leads to a striking epistemological thesis: no presentation is fully adequate to its object. Since the number of parts or aspects of an object is boundless and the number of elements in a content is finite, the content always underdetermines the object. Twardowski thereby anticipates a theme central to later phenomenology and analytic philosophy of mind: intentional content is selective, perspectival, and partial. The mind is directed to objects, but never captures them exhaustively in a single act.

This point also helps avoid two extremes. Against idealism, the object is not reducible to the content. Against naïve direct realism, the object is not simply given in full. The mental act reaches outward, but it does so through a structured and partial content.

6. Classification of mental phenomena: presentation, judgment, feeling, will

Twardowski’s mature classification of mental phenomena has four basic classes: presentations, judgments, feelings, and manifestations of will. This is a significant modification of Brentano, who had classified mental phenomena into presentations, judgments, and phenomena of love and hate. Twardowski keeps the Brentanian distinction between presentations and judgments, but rejects the unity of feeling and willing.

Presentations include images and concepts. They are the most basic mental phenomena because other acts are founded on them. Concepts, in Twardowski’s later work, are presentations with well-defined content, especially where the content is fixed by definition. This is why his theory of concepts belongs simultaneously to psychology, epistemology, and logic: a concept is a kind of mental presentation, but its definition and use raise questions of objectivity and meaning.

Judgments are sui generis acts. Twardowski follows the idiogenetic theory of judgment: judging is not merely combining presentations, but accepting or rejecting an object presented at the basis of the judgment. The judgment “A exists” accepts A; the judgment “A does not exist” rejects A. This theory is important for his account of truth, negative existential judgments, and logical objectivity.

Feelings are also sui generis. Twardowski argues against reducing feelings to sensations or physiological reactions. Feelings have quality, degree, duration, intentionality, and a basis in presentation. Their qualities are pleasure and displeasure; they can vary in liveliness or intensity; they occur in time; and they are directed toward objects apprehended as pleasant or unpleasant. A feeling is therefore not just bodily arousal but an intentional mental phenomenon founded on presentation.

The will is likewise sui generis. Volition cannot be reduced to presentation, judgment, or feeling. Its distinctive phenomenology is resolution: one decides that something is to be real, or not real, in the future through one’s own action. The object of will is not merely represented, judged true or false, or felt as pleasant or unpleasant; it is grasped under the practical mode of something to be brought about or prevented by oneself.

Desire, interestingly, is not a fundamental class. Twardowski treats desires as composite phenomena involving presentations, feelings, and judgments. In his analysis, desire involves the presentation of an object, a supposition about its reality, a belief that its existence or non-existence would bring pleasure or distress, and a feeling based on these components. This makes his theory unusually fine-grained: feeling and will are primitive modes, but desire is a structured hybrid.

7. Judgment, truth, and the anti-relativist theory of mind

Twardowski’s theory of mind cannot be separated from his theory of judgment. In On the So-Called Relative Truths, he argues that truth-bearers are judgments, not sentences. A sentence may appear to change truth-value because it is elliptical, indexical, or context-sensitive, but the judgment it expresses is complete and unambiguous. Thus, truth is absolute: if a judgment is true, it is true independently of circumstance, time, and place.

This has a philosophy-of-mind consequence. A judgment is not merely an inscription, utterance, or linguistic form. It is a mental-logical product that can be expressed in language but is not identical with the sentence. Sentences are public vehicles; judgments are the cognitive products they express. Relativism arises when one confuses the vehicle with the judgment expressed by it.

Twardowski’s anti-relativism also matters historically. His work on absolute truth became important for Polish discussions of truth and influenced later work in the Lvov-Warsaw School, including debates connected with Leśniewski, Łukasiewicz, Kotarbiński, and eventually Tarski’s intellectual environment.

8. Actions and products: from mental acts to public meaning

Twardowski’s 1912 distinction between actions and products is one of his most important mature contributions. An action is a process; a product is what results from it. Running produces a run; judging produces a judgment; speaking produces speech. He distinguishes physical, psychical, and psychophysical actions and products. A judgment as psychological action is a judging; a judgment as logical product is the product of that judging.

This distinction is his mature route out of psychologism. Early Twardowski identified the meaning of a name with the psychological content of a presentation, which made semantics dependent on individual mental acts. Later, he marks the difference between psychological acts and logical products. Logic is not about the empirical processes of thinking as such; it is about products of thinking considered with respect to truth and inferential relations. This is why he says the demarcation between actions and products helps liberate logic from psychological accretions.

The same distinction explains public meaning. A spoken sentence is a psychophysical product that expresses a mental product, such as a judgment. A written sentence is an enduring psychophysical product that can preserve and re-evoke similar mental products in other persons or at other times. Meaning, in the mature account, is not simply a private mental token; it is what we arrive at by abstracting common attributes from many individual mental products evoked by signs.

This is a subtle middle position. Twardowski does not become a Fregean Platonist about meanings, but he also does not remain a simple psychologistic mentalist. Meanings are rooted in mental products, stabilized through psychophysical signs, and treated abstractly through common features. Contemporary act-based theories of semantic content have rediscovered Twardowski’s action-product distinction as an alternative to treating propositions as fully mind-independent and language-independent entities.

9. The soul and incorporeal mind

Twardowski’s explicit writings on the soul must be integrated carefully. They are not merely incidental, because they address the subject of mental phenomena, the unity of consciousness, the soul-body relation, and immortality. But they also do not dominate the technical apparatus of his intentionality theory, which can be developed at the level of acts, contents, objects, and products without first settling all metaphysical questions about immortality.

In Remarks on the Classification of Views on the Relation between the Soul and the Body, Twardowski treats the soul-body problem as a classificatory and terminological issue, involving distinctions such as actuality theory, substantialist theory, worldview, and terminological confusion. This already shows that he did not approach the problem merely as a crude choice between “materialism” and “dualism”; he was sensitive to how classifications of soul-body views can become misleading.

In Metaphysics of the Soul, however, Twardowski makes a stronger metaphysical claim. He defines “soul,” in the relevant sense, as a non-material subject or non-corporeal substance of mental phenomena. On that definition, materialists and Fechnerian parallelists lack a genuine concept of soul, because they either identify the subject of mental phenomena with something corporeal or treat the soul as only a homogeneous whole or succession of mental phenomena.

His main opponent is the bundle theory of the self, associated in the text with Hume, Fechner, and related views. Twardowski asks whether mental life can be only a succession or group of phenomena unified by self-awareness and memory. His answer is no. He admits he cannot prove the self’s existence in the ordinary inferential way, but says the existence of the self is immediately obvious. His “dialogue with a group of mental phenomena” tries to show that if the self were only a group of phenomena, there would be an infinite regress in explaining how the group knows which phenomena belong to it.

The metaphysical conclusion is that mental phenomena belong to a subject as accidents belong to a substance. Twardowski then argues that this subject is single and indivisible. His central argument appeals to comparison: if a visual impression, an auditory impression, and the act comparing them belonged to different partial subjects, comparison would be impossible. Since we do compare mental phenomena, the subject of the phenomena compared and the subject of comparison must be the same; hence the subject of mental phenomena is one and not composed of parts.

This gives Twardowski a robust anti-materialist argument in the soul writings. If the subject of mental phenomena is indivisible, then it cannot be the brain conceived as a composite physical object. He explicitly rejects views that deny the subject’s existence or seek it in the human brain, and he treats those rejections as creating a “surplus in favor of immortality.”

His position on immortality is nuanced but ultimately affirmative in Metaphysics of the Soul. In Contemporary Philosophy on the Immortality of the Soul, he warns that dualism by itself does not prove immortality: if the soul begins in time, then philosophy cannot exclude its ending in time without additional theological assumptions; only a Platonic preexistence thesis gives dualism a strictly philosophical route to immortality.

Yet in Metaphysics of the Soul he proceeds further. Since the subject or self is indivisible and cannot arise through natural forces in the way composite physical products arise, he argues that it is eternal; properly interpreted, creation and preexistence need not conflict, because creation by God is not a temporal event. He concludes that the soul, being eternal, is immortal.

The philosophy-of-mind upshot is this: Twardowski’s core descriptive psychology can be read without adopting the full metaphysics of immortality, but his own broader view includes a substantialist account of the self. The self is not a bundle, not the brain, and not a mere grammatical fiction. It is the non-corporeal subject of mental acts. This places Twardowski partly within the older metaphysics of soul, but with a modern analytic-descriptive method: he argues from the unity, comparison, and ownership of mental phenomena rather than from purely theological doctrine.

10. The systematic philosophy of mind extracted from Twardowski

A comprehensive Twardowskian philosophy of mind can be stated in the following theses.

First, the mark of the mental is intentionality. Mental phenomena are directed toward objects. This directedness is not reducible to bodily stimulation or linguistic behavior. It is a structural feature of presentations, judgments, feelings, and volitions.

Second, every mental act has a threefold structure: act, content, and object. The act occurs; the content exists dependently within the act; the object is what the act is directed toward and need not exist. This triad is Twardowski’s most important technical contribution to philosophy of mind.

Third, the mind is world-directed, not merely content-directed. We think about objects through contents; we do not normally think about contents themselves unless we perform second-order acts. This prevents the mind from becoming a closed container of inner images.

Fourth, intentionality outruns existence. Fictional, impossible, and nonexistent objects can be intended, named, judged, and denied. Thought is therefore not limited to successful reference to existing things.

Fifth, mental content is partial and perspectival. Contents present selected marks of objects; they never exhaust the object. This gives Twardowski a proto-phenomenological theory of perspectival presentation.

Sixth, mental life is plural in kind. Presenting, judging, feeling, and willing are irreducible modes. In particular, judgment is not a mere combination of representations, feeling is not mere sensation or physiology, and will is not merely desire or affect.

Seventh, desire is composite. It involves presentation, affective valuation, and judgment-like assumptions about reality and pleasure or distress. This makes Twardowski’s theory of conation more differentiated than many simple pleasure-based or will-based theories.

Eighth, inner experience is the epistemic foundation of psychology, but not the whole of psychological method. Inner perception is self-evident; inner observation is fallible; psychology must use memory, abstraction, comparison, and, where appropriate, empirical and experimental procedures.

Ninth, logical and semantic objectivity require distinguishing acts from products. Psychological acts occur in time; their products can be considered logically, expressed in signs, preserved in writing, and abstracted into shared meanings.

Tenth, truth belongs primarily to judgments, not sentences. Sentences can be ambiguous, elliptical, and context-sensitive; judgments, when properly identified, are complete truth-bearers. This gives Twardowski an anti-relativist theory of truth rooted in a philosophy of cognitive acts and products.

Eleventh, the self is not a mere bundle of mental phenomena. In his metaphysics of the soul, Twardowski treats the self as the subject of mental phenomena, argues against reducing it to a group or succession of experiences, and defends its unity and indivisibility.

Twelfth, Twardowski’s overall position is anti-reductionist but not anti-scientific. He resists reducing mind to physiology, language, or behavior, yet he insists on clarity, empirical grounding, analysis, classification, and methodological rigor.

11. Historical significance in the development of philosophy of mind

Twardowski’s historical importance is greater than his relatively small published corpus might suggest. He is one of the key mediators between Brentano’s rediscovery of intentionality and later twentieth-century philosophy. Through the content-object distinction, he helped shape Husserl’s phenomenology, Meinong’s theory of objects, and debates about nonexistence, meaning, and reference. Through the action-product distinction, he helped formulate a non-psychologistic but non-Platonist account of meaning. Through the Lvov-Warsaw School, he influenced a philosophical culture that prized clarity, logic, anti-relativism, and rigorous analysis.

Within philosophy of mind specifically, he occupies a position between classical soul metaphysics and modern theories of intentional content. He inherits the older concern with the soul as subject of mental life, but his most enduring contribution is not a Cartesian substance dualism. It is an analytic theory of intentional acts and their objects. He transforms the problem of mind from “What substance is the mind?” into a more fine-grained set of questions: What is an act? What is its content? What is its object? How can one think about what does not exist? What is the difference between judging, presenting, feeling, and willing? How can private mental products become public meanings?

Compared with Descartes, Twardowski is less centered on epistemic doubt and more centered on intentional structure. Compared with Hume, he rejects the bundle theory of self. Compared with Brentano, he clarifies the object/content ambiguity and expands the classification of mental phenomena. Compared with Husserl, he remains closer to empirical descriptive psychology and does not make the transcendental turn. Compared with Frege, he develops a non-Platonist semantics grounded in mental products and signs. Compared with later behaviorism and physicalism, he preserves the autonomy of mental phenomena and inner experience.

The most accurate label for the extracted view is therefore: intentional realist descriptive psychology with a non-Platonist theory of meaning, anti-psychologistic logic, and a substantialist metaphysics of the self. It is realist because objects are not reduced to contents; intentionalist because all mental life is object-directed; descriptive-psychological because analysis begins from inner experience; anti-psychologistic because logic and truth cannot be reduced to empirical acts of thinking; and substantialist about the self because, in his soul writings, Twardowski explicitly rejects the reduction of the self to a bundle of mental phenomena.

12. Critical tensions

There are several tensions in the system. The first is the tension between early psychologism and later anti-psychologism. In Content and Object, meaning is tied to psychological content; in Actions and Products, Twardowski moves toward an abstract but still non-Platonist account of meaning. This is not an outright contradiction, but a development from private mental content toward publicly stabilized products and abstracted common features.

The second tension concerns nonexistent objects. Twardowski’s “no objectless presentations” thesis gives a powerful account of empty and fictional thought, but it also risks admitting too many objects. The theory’s force depends on accepting that every categorematic name names something, even when that something does not exist. This is precisely the family of views later challenged by Russell.

The third tension concerns introspection. Twardowski gives inner perception foundational status, but he also acknowledges the difficulty of observing one’s own mental facts without altering them. His method therefore requires a careful balance between self-evidence, memory, comparison, and public signs.

The fourth tension concerns the soul. The intentionality theory does not require a full doctrine of immortality, but the soul writings defend a strong metaphysical thesis about the self as indivisible, non-corporeal subject. A contemporary interpreter can extract Twardowski’s philosophy of mind at two levels: a minimal level consisting of act-content-object analysis, taxonomy, introspection, and action-product semantics; and a maximal level that adds the substantial soul and its immortality. The maximal view is Twardowski’s own in the early soul texts, but the minimal view explains why his work remained influential even among philosophers who did not accept his metaphysics of immortality.

Final synthesis

Twardowski’s philosophy of mind is a theory of intentional mental life as structured, object-directed, internally accessible, and expressible in public signs. Its basic unit is not the isolated sensation, the brain state, or the linguistic sentence, but the intentional act: presenting, judging, feeling, or willing. Each act has a content and an object; the content mediates access, while the object is what the act is about. The mind can intend nonexistent and contradictory objects because intentionality is broader than existence. Mental life is known primarily through inner experience, but psychology must analyze, classify, and generalize from psychic facts. Logic and truth emerge from mental products, yet cannot be reduced to empirical psychology. Finally, behind the flow of acts Twardowski’s metaphysical writings posit a unified, indivisible, non-corporeal subject: the soul or self.

The result is one of the most important transitional philosophies of mind between nineteenth-century descriptive psychology and twentieth-century analytic philosophy and phenomenology. Twardowski preserves the old question of the soul, but he retools the study of mind around intentionality, content, judgment, meaning, and acts. That is why his importance lies not only in the Lvov-Warsaw School, but in the broader formation of modern philosophy of mind itself.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
